chapters.title: Being Asian American often means feeling unseen
chapters.text:
There are many ethnic groups in the U.S. experiencing racism and unequal treatment. The Asian American community is often the group that doesn’t get mentioned as much as perhaps the Black community or the Hispanic community.

Does that mean they’re not subjected to racism as much as other groups? Not at all. The difference here is that Asian Americans are somehow invisible yet still divisive amongst many White Americans at the same time.

Asian Americans are not talked about as much as many other ethnic groups in the U.S. This can lead to Asian Americans feeling invisible.

According to Cathy Park Hong, “Asian Americans inhabit a vague purgatorial status ... distrusted by African Americans, ignored by whites, unless we’re being used by whites to keep the black man down.” Asian Americans are constantly struggling to work out where they fit in, often toiling and compensating too much just to be noticed by those around them. For many years, Cathy Park Hong struggled with her identity, and she often felt invisible. She worked hard to prove herself yet never really knew what she had to do to be accepted.

Hong’s parents traveled to the U.S. from Seoul, Korea. Hong herself was born in Koreatown, Los Angeles. Throughout her childhood, there were many ups and downs, including a distinct lack of money. However, her father eventually bought a business that funded Hong’s education. Hong shares how her father’s experiences of racial injustice have shaped the way he sees those around him today. For her father, every small thing comes down to the fact that he is Asian and not White. This mistrust has been born out of years of unequal treatment.

The question of racial identity can bedevil the children of Asian immigrants. ~ Cathy Park Hong

chapters.title: The history of Asian Americans shows racism in abundance
chapters.text:
Hong has tried to talk to White people about racism many times and has always found it a frustrating and tiring process. It’s challenging to explain a situation to someone who has never experienced it and never will. The reality of a person belonging to an ethnic group is utterly different from a White American’s reality, dating back hundreds of years into history.

The experiences of an Asian American are so different from those of a White American. Explaining racism to a White person is extremely difficult due to a lack of understanding.

Books, films, politics, and even history, all focus on being White. That can often lead to someone who isn’t White feeling left out, invisible, and that their culture or history isn’t as important. All of this leaves Asian Americans questioning their identity and feeling as if they just don’t exist.

Patiently educating a clueless White person about race is draining. It takes all your powers of persuasion. Because it’s more than a chat about race. It’s ontological. It’s like explaining to a person why you exist, or why you feel pain, or why your reality is distinct from their reality. Except it’s even trickier than that. Because the person has all of Western history, politics, literature, and mass culture on their side, proving that you don’t exist. ~ Cathy Park Hong

When you look through history at when immigration from Asia began, it links back to China. Countless Chinese citizens were sent over to the U.S. to work in plantations after abolishing the slave trade to build railways and roads across America. Despite their unquestionable hard work, they never received any credit. They were expected to keep their heads down and get on with it. Of course, this was dangerous work, and many immigrants died during the building process of railways. None of this was ever reported or even mentioned.

The fact that Asian immigration history is linked so heavily to China makes it hard for Hong to really connect. She is Korean, not Chinese, and the culture is different. This makes it even harder to solidify her identity.

Chinese immigrants found their lives worsening in the U.S. during the 1800s when there was a massive backlash against anyone deemed different. They were stuck in their houses, fearing violence if they were found outside. The U.S. continued its racist track in 1882 when they banned Chinese citizens from even entering the country and then extending it to the whole of the Asian continent in 1917. None of this helped with anti-Asian sentiments in the U.S., even today.

There were several immigration bans on Asians entering the U.S. during the 1800s and early 1900s.

The U.S. government rescinded the ban in 1965, and this is when Hong’s parents took their chances and moved over to the U.S. However, even then, they were expected to work harder than anyone else, keep their heads down, and not complain.

chapters.title: The American Airlines incident shocked and highlighted racism in the U.S.
chapters.text:
On April 9, 2017, an incident involving a Vietnamese man on a United Airlines flight shocked the world. This incident highlighted the poor treatment of Asian Americans in the States and broadcast across the globe. The images were nothing short of shocking.

David Dao was a 69-year-old man of Vietnamese origin. He was small and slight and was simply sitting on the plane, having booked a ticket and paid for it in full. He had every reason to be on that flight, just as every other person there. After boarding, the airline staff asked for volunteers to move flights due to overcrowding. Of course, nobody volunteered because everyone needed to travel, and they’d paid for their ticket.

David Dao’s forceful removal from a United Airlines flight was a harsh reminder of the poor treatment of Asian Americans.

The airline staff then had to randomly choose someone to eject from the plane and that person was Dao. Rather than politely asking him if he would mind giving up his seat and then listening to his reasons when he told them that he couldn’t and that he had to get home, they forcefully removed him from the plane. Dao was dragged unconscious from the plane, his glasses knocked from his face, and blood pouring out of his mouth were broadcast globally.

It’s evident that the only reason he was chosen and treated in that violent way was that he was foreign. As such, he was considered to be of “less value”. You have to question whether they would have treated a White American in that way, and the answer would very likely be “no”.

Would a White American have been dragged unconscious from a plane? Very unlikely. Dao was chosen because of his foreign appearance.

chapters.title: Asian Americans are less “seen” but expected to work harder
chapters.text:
Hong often worries that the Asian American identity will disappear into oblivion before long. When you look at polls and forms that need to be filled in, there is no box for Asian Americans in the ethnicity section. Instead, it simply says “other”. Besides, you will struggle to find hard statistics on any type of mistreatment of Asian Americans in workplace settings or other statics, such as domestic violence and unemployment.

The Asian American identity is being eroded. It’s hard to know who you are when your culture and identity are pushed to one side.

Instead, Asian Americans receive the status of “other”, a seemingly dark, black hole that groups together minorities that don’t get the same airtime. This is all very surprising when you consider the millions of Asian Americans who call the U.S. home.

Instead, this large group is expected to abide by the law, work hard, say nothing, and keep ticking along. Hong struggles with a lack of identity but also worries about what this means for the future.

Of course, all this does is cause Asian Americans to question who they are, where they came from, and their future chances in life. Constantly working harder than anyone else to prove themselves and be seen never seems to work, especially when they always have to tick the “other” box.

Asian Americans often work harder than most, yet rarely receive the financial gains or the respect that should come along with it.

chapters.title: The L.A. riots fundamentally altered Korean Americans’ course of life in the U.S.
chapters.text:
Born in Koreatown, Hong has an affinity for the area, even though she doesn’t live in that part of L.A. anymore. During her childhood, she remembers that many people avoided Koreatown because it had a reputation for crime. Yet, all she remembers are people who joined together, shared experiences, smiled, and created a sense of community.

Despite her happy memories, Koreatown was all but destroyed during the L.A. riots in 1992. As the riots spread across the city, they eventually moved into Koreatown. They fanned the flames of racial divides even more. Korean businesses were wiped out, leaving many families struggling to make ends meet.

The 1992 L.A. riots left Koreatown in a severe state of disrepair and subsequent poverty. The government never showed interest in rebuilding it.

Following the riots, thousands of Korean immigrants demonstrated and asked for financial help to rebuild their businesses, but this fell on deaf government ears. Many families struggled even more, and many decided to leave their homes and move from the U.S. altogether.

For immigrants from Korea, the riots and widespread violence triggered haunting memories from the past. On top of the fact that their businesses were burned to the ground and no help was in sight, the situation darkened significantly, many Koreans felt utterly lacking in value in the government’s eyes.

Koreatown was never wholly rebuilt and since that day has appeared less than its former glory.

The lack of interest in Korean businesses left many immigrants feeling lost. This further eroded away their sense of identity.

chapters.title: Childhood as an Asian American is often difficult
chapters.text:
Hong is now a mother herself, and when she looks back on her childhood, she feels sad about her lack of happy memories. She had many White friends, but when she visited their homes for play dates, she always felt like she didn’t fit in and that her friends had far more than she ever had. She is doing everything she can to create a happier childhood for her daughter.

Reading to my daughter, I see my own youth drifting away while hers attaches firmly to this country. I am not passing down happy memories of my own so much as I am staging happy memories for her. ~ Cathy Park Hong

Throughout her childhood, Hong remembers feeling envious. She recalls several situations when her family was left humiliated and feeling not good enough. They worked hard but never got what they deserved, simply disappearing into the background.

An Asian American’s childhood is often very different from that of a White person’s. Hong remembers feeling envious a lot of the time.

Hong becomes angry that many Asian Americans feel they can’t speak up against the repression they have experienced. It’s a vicious cycle; if they speak up, they’re told that America gave them nothing but opportunities to build a prosperous life. However, the truth is that Asian Americans worked harder than most, keeping everything to themselves and hoping things would improve. Besides, by not talking about the treatment they’re subjected to, they’re feeding the myth that Asian Americans simply don’t speak up because of their culture.

Many Asian Americans don’t speak up because they don’t feel they would be listened to.

chapters.title: The rise of hate crimes haunts minority communities in the U.S.
chapters.text:
Hate crimes began to rise in the U.S. after Donald Trump was elected President. Of course, these types of crimes happened before this time too. Still, upon that election, many ethnic groups became fearful of what may come their way.

Hate crimes and feeling pushed aside in life often trigger past experience memories for immigrants who fled difficult situations in their home countries. Of course, when children hear their parents talking in a racist way, this also translates into the playground. Children from ethnic groups find themselves bullied and targeted in places they should feel safe and supported.

When parents speak in a racist way in front of children, they repeat these words in the playground, targeting children of ethnic minorities.

Hong remembers being bullied at school and being told that she should “go home”. She also struggled with her English until she was around seven years old, mostly because she was simply surrounded by Korean language at home. When Hong looks at the situation in the U.S. for children these days, she sees no progress and a static situation that causes little hope for the future.

During the Trump presidency, hate crimes began to rise in the U.S. and racist behavior was blindingly apparent.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Racism in the U.S. is a real problem. Many ethnic groups call the U.S. home, having ancestors who traveled there many years ago searching for a better life. What many of those groups found instead was a profoundly racist view towards immigration that spreads to this very day.

Of course, it’s important to point out that not every single person in the U.S. is racist. That’s not the truth at all, yet there is a powerful element within the American mindset and racism within crucial systems, such as policing, government policies, and even healthcare.

Throughout her life, Cathy Park Hong has experienced a different reality compared to a White American. She can see the situation through different eyes, having faced challenges every day. It’s essential that as a society, we listen to Asian Americans and other ethnic groups and hear their opinions, their experiences, and their feelings. Suppose we are ever to banish racism from society as a whole. In that case, we need to learn to fully understand the issue and have a strong desire to do better.

Perhaps the most worrying thing about Hong’s experiences is that she has struggled so badly with her identity since being a child. This is something that she explains many Asian Americans feel. The Asian culture is a highly diverse, rich, and wonderful blend. This should be embraced as part of American society, allowing Asian Americans to share their culture and enrich their own. This shouldn’t be viewed as something “different” or “suspicious”; it should be something to learn about, appreciate, and embrace.

Try this:

1. Many people simply don’t understand the complexities of racism fully, yet they really want to learn. Learning means you can do better, and you can encourage everyone around you to do better, too. Look for more books on the Headway app that can help you explore the subject.

2. Not every Asian American will want to sit down with you and talk about the issue of racism. If this is the case, you must respect their privacy. However, asking questions and learning is a way to improve. Simply make sure that you’re respectful when asking anything about this sensitive subject.

3. Every person in society needs to hold themselves accountable. This doesn’t mean that you’re racist; it means that you’re an ally in the struggle. You want to help banish the unfair treatment of ethnic groups. Assess your past behavior and look at where you can change and improve.

Read it till the end?
